transcript: Welcome to Lesson 6.8, Segmentation and Personalization Strategies. You now have all the foundational skills for email and SMS campaigns. You understand deliverability, the four campaign types, how to write copy that converts, how to design emails for performance, how to build campaigns in Go High level, and the compliance rules that govern SMS. There is one more strategic layer that transforms everything you have built from good to exceptional. Segmentation and personalization. Segmentation is the decision to stop trading your entire list the same way. Personalization is the execution of that decision. Messages that feel individually written rather than broadcast to thousands. Together, they are responsible for the largest single performance gap. between average marketing automation and genuinely effective marketing automation. By the end of this lesson, you will have a complete framework for segmenting any contact list and personalizing any campaign. Let's get into it. Let me show you the performance gap before anything else, because the numbers make the case more powerfully than any framework. Two campaigns, same list, same offer, same send time. The first sends the same email to everyone, an unsegmented broadcast, generic subject line, generic body copy, generic CTA. The result, 14% open rate, 1.2% CTR, high unsubscribes. The second segments the list before sending. Warm leads in active nurture receive a different email from cold leads who have been inactive for 90 days. The warm leads receive a benefit-focused message relevant to where they are in their journey. The inactive contacts receive a re-engagement message. The result, 38% open rate, 8.4% CTR, low on subscribes. That gap from 14% to 38% open rate is not the result of better copy. The copy was similar, it is the result of relevance. when a message feels like it was written for you specifically. Addressing your situation, your stage, your behavior. You open it when it feels like it was written for no one in particular. You ignore it. Segmentation is not a technical feature you toggle on. It is a strategic decision to treat different people differently. That decision, made before every campaign you send, is one of the highest leverage actions available to you as a marketing automation specialist. There are four types of segmentation, and each uses different data to divide your contact list into more precise groups. Type number one is demographic segmentation. This divides contacts by who they are, age, location, job title, industry, company size, income level. An example, Sending a B2B services offer only to contacts whose job title contains manager or director In Go High Level, demographic data is stored in custom fields on the contact record and filtered using those field values Type number two is behavioral segmentation This divides contacts by what they have done Emails opened Links clicked Pages visited Forms submitted Purchases made An example, sending a follow-up email only to contacts who clicked the pricing page link in the previous email. In Go High Level, behavioral segmentation is implemented through tags. When a contact takes a specific action, a workflow automatically applies a tag. That tag becomes the filter criterion for the next campaign. Type number three is psychographic segmentation. This divides contacts by how they think. Their goals, challenges, values, motivations, and buying objections. An example, if contacts answered a qualification survey question asking what matters most to you, growing revenue or getting more free time, contacts who answered differently received different nurture content. In Go High Level, psychographic data is captured through survey responses or quiz results stored in custom fields. Type number four is lifecycle stage segmentation. This divides contacts by where they are in the customer journey. New lead, active nurture, sales qualified, current customer, inactive. An example, sending a re-engagement campaign only to contacts who have not opened an email in 90 days and are not current customers In Go High Level lifecycle stages are represented by pipeline stages and contact tags that update automatically as contacts progress Behavioral and lifecycle stage segmentation deliver the highest performance lifts for most campaigns Start with these two before adding demographic or psychographic layers. Now let me show you exactly how segmentation applies to the four campaign types from Lesson 6.2. because each campaign type has a different segmentation profile. Welcome campaigns target lifecycle stage only. Brand new leads who submitted a form in the last 24 hours. The GHL filter is simple. Tag equals new lead. The personalization opportunity is the lead magnet topic. If they downloaded a fitness guide versus a marketing guide, the welcome email should reference that specifically. Critically, exclude anyone who is already a customer or who has been on your list for more than 24 hours. Nurture campaigns target behavioral and life cycle together. Contacts who have completed the welcome sequence have not purchased and have been on the list for 7 to 90 days. The GHL filters are Tag equals welcome complete No tag equals customer They since opt-in between 7 and 90. The personalization opportunity is the content they engaged with. If they clicked a case study link, send more proof-based content in the next nurture email. Promotional campaigns target behavioral and life cycle simultaneously. Warm leads who have shown buying signals. GHL filters. Tag equals nurture complete or tag equals click pricing page. No tag equals customer. Personalization can extend to offer tier. A solopreneur's segment gets a different framing of the same offer than a team of 10 segment. Re-engagement campaigns target behavioral specifically. No email opens or clicks in 60 to 90 days. GHL filters, last email opened more than 60 days ago, no tag equals customer, no tag equals SMS unsubscribed. Personalization references the original opt-in. You downloaded our guide on X, here is what you might have missed. The exclusion filters in every segment are just as important as the inclusion filters. A customer receiving a Join Our Program promotional email feels overlooked. Getting exclusions right is what makes your contacts feel understood rather than processed. Here is what segmentation looks like inside GoHighLevel's contacts panel. The filter sidebar on the left allows you to stack multiple conditions simultaneously. In this example, three filters are applied. Tag equals nurture complete, tag does not include customer, and last activity is more than 60 days ago. Together, these three conditions define a re-engagement segment. Contacts who completed the nurture sequence are not yet customers and have gone cold. The result, 47 contacts matched this segment out of a total contact list of several hundred. Only these 47 received a re-engagement campaign. Everyone else is excluded automatically. Notice the Smart List Save option visible in the panel. Once you have defined a segment this precisely, you can save it as a Smart List in Go High Level. That same segment updates dynamically. As new contacts meet the criteria, they are automatically added. As contacts convert to customers or re-engage, they are automatically removed. You build the filter once and it maintains itself. Every tag visible in these filters was applied automatically by a Go high-level workflow. No manual tagging was required. When a contact completed the welcome sequence, a workflow applied the welcome complete tag. When they did not open for 60 days, a workflow identified them and applied the inactive tag. Segmentation at scale is only possible when tag application is fully automated. You will build those tag applying workflows in Module 7. Segmentation decides who receives your message. Personalization determines how relevant that message feels when they read it. And genuine personalization goes far deeper than inserting a first name. There are three levels of personalization. Surface personalization, using the contact's first name in the subject line or greeting, is the baseline that every email platform has supported for years. The performance lift is minimal because every brand does it. It no longer signals that a message was written for you specifically. Situational personalization references the contact specific situation, behavior, or stage. You downloaded our fitness guide last week Here is the next step situational It tells the reader that you know something real about them This level produces a significant performance lift because it feels individually written Predictive personalization anticipates what the contact needs based on their pattern of behavior. Most people who completed day three of the guide struggle with this one thing, here is the fix, speaks to where they are in a journey and what challenge commonly emerges there. This level produces the highest performance lift because it feels like reading their mind. In GoHighLevel, there are five personalization dimensions you can apply. Name and identity tokens, first name, company name, are available on every contact record. Lead source references how they found you. Since you came to us through our Instagram post, Stored in a custom field, lead magnet or opt-in topic references what they originally downloaded Captured as a tag or custom field from the opt-in form Engagement behavior references what they clicked Captured automatically as a behavioral tag by your workflow And life cycle stage acknowledges where they are in the journey You have been with us for 30 days now Here is what most people discover at this stage Here is the difference in practice. A generic offer email says, Hi First Name, we have a new offer for you this week. Our program is 20% off. Click here to learn more. A personalized offer email says, Hi Marco, you downloaded our 5-day home workout guide 12 days ago. Most people who finish it ask the same question next. How do I make this sustainable? That is exactly what our 12-week program answers. This week only, it is 20% off. Same offer, same discount, completely different conversion rate. Because the second version references real behavior and answers the real next question the reader has. Segmentation is most powerful when it is planned before campaigns are built, not retrofitted after. A segment architecture is the map you create before opening GoHighLevel that defines every segment, every tag, and every personalization opportunity for your entire campaign system. Step number one is to map your customer journey stages. List every distinct state a contact can be in. New lead, active nurture, warm lead, current customer, inactive, re-engaged. Each stage becomes a pipeline stage or a tag and go high level. Step number two is to define your key behavioral tags. What actions signal interest, readiness, or disengagement? Each behavior that matters becomes a tag. Examples, downloaded guide, clicked pricing, attended webinar, opened three emails consecutively, inactive 60 days. Each tag is applied automatically by a workflow trigger when the behavior occurs. Step number three is to define your campaign segments. For each campaign type, welcome, nurture, promotional, re-engagement, write the exact filter criteria. Which tags must be present? Which must be absent? Which pipeline stage? What time window since opt-in? Each specification becomes a saved smart list in Go High-Level Contacts. Step number four is to map personalization opportunities per segment. For each segment, identify which personalization dimensions apply. What do you know about these contacts that can be referenced naturally in the copy? List the available tokens and situational references for each segment before writing a word of copy. Step number five is to build, test, and refine. Create your smart lists in GHL, send the test sent to verify the right contacts are included, and monitor performance differences between segments. The architecture is a living plan. Refine it as you learn what your specific audience responds to. A segment architecture built before campaign creation means every campaign has a predefined, verified audience. You stop asking who should receive this and start asking what is the best message for this specific group. That mental shift is the difference between email marketing and email conversation. Let me close with 7 segmentation mistakes that consistently undermine campaign performance and the specific fix for each. Mistake number 1. Sending to your entire list for every campaign. Promotional emails to new leads accelerate unsubscribes. Re-engagement emails to active contacts feel confusing. Define the target segment before every single campaign without exception. Mistake number two, using only inclusion filters with no exclusion filters. A customer receiving your Join Our Program email feels unseen Every segment definition must specify what to exclude as precisely as what to include Mistake number three. Using only demographic segmentation while ignoring behavioral data. Demographics tell you who someone is. Behavior tells you what they want right now. Fix. Layer behavioral tags onto any demographic segment. Behavior is always the stronger signal for campaign timing. Mistake number four. Over-segmenting into too many tiny lists. Segments below 50 contacts rarely produce statistically meaningful data. Fix. Start with three to five core segments per campaign type and consolidate before expanding. Mistake number five. Personalizing the subject line but leaving the body copy generic. A personalized subject line that opens into generic body copy breaks the promise of relevance immediately. Fix If you personalize the subject line, the first sentence of the body must reflect the same personalization signal. Mistake number six. Never updating segments as contact status changes. A contact who converted to a customer six months ago but still sits in your warm lead segment receives irrelevant campaigns indefinitely. Fix. Configure go-high-level workflows to automatically update tags and pipeline stages the moment contact status changes. Segments must reflect real-time state. Mistake number seven. Building segments manually instead of automating tag application. Manual segmentation cannot scale beyond a few hundred contacts. Fix Every tag that defines a segment is applied automatically by a GHL workflow trigger. Behavior happens, workflow fires, tag applied, segment updates. No manual work required. Fixing mistakes 1, 2, and 7, sending to everyone, missing exclusions, and managing segments manually produces the highest immediate performance improvement for any email and SMS program that is currently underperforming, let's lock in what you learned in this lesson. Segmentation is the decision to send different messages to different people. It is the single highest leverage performance variable in email and SMS marketing, producing open rate improvements of 20% or more compared to unsegmented broadcasts to the same list. The four segmentation types are demographic, behavioral, psychographic, and lifestyle stage. Behavioral and lifecycle stage segmentation deliver the highest immediate performance lifts and should be the foundation of every campaign segment you define. Go high-level implements segmentation through tags applied automatically by workflow triggers and pipeline stages. Contacts move through segments automatically based on their behavior. No manual segmentation required when workflows are built correctly. Real personalization goes far beyond first name. Situational personalization references what the contact downloaded, clicked, or engaged with. Predictive personalization anticipates what they need next based on behavioral patterns. Both produce significantly higher performance than surface-level name personalization alone. A segment architecture is built before campaigns. mapping lifecycle stages, defining behavioral tags, specifying segment filters, identifying personalization opportunities per segment, then building and testing inside GoHighLevel's smart lists. The three highest impact segmentation fixes are always defining a target segment before sending, rather than broadcasting to everyone, always including exclusion filters alongside inclusion filters, and automating all tag applications through GHL workflows rather than managing segments manually. Coming up next is Lesson 6.9, Email and SMS Analytics, What to Track and Why. Now that you know how to segment your list and personalize your messages, Lesson 6.9 teaches you how to measure whether all of it is working. You will learn the specific metrics to track for email and SMS campaigns beyond open rate and CTR, how to read GoHighLevel's reporting dashboard to identify what is working and what needs fixing, and how to use data to make optimization decisions that continuously improve performance across every campaign type. Segmentation tells you who to send to. Personalization tells you what to say. Analytics tells you what happened and what to do next. Lesson 6.9 closes the loop on the complete email and SMS campaign system you have been building throughout Module 6. Let's continue to Lesson 6.9.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a slide from a presentation on **Segmentation and Personalization Strategies**, focusing on the importance of sending the right message to the right person at the right time. The slide is divided into two main sections: **What You'll Learn** and a flowchart illustrating the process.

**What You'll Learn:**

*   What segmentation is and not only sending the same message to everyone
*   How campaign segmentation performance measurements, behavioral, psychographic, and lifecycle stages
*   How Go-to-Market uses tags and propensities to implement segmentation successfully
*   How to personalize beyond the name with dynamic, automation-based messaging
*   How to build a segmentation architecture before launching any campaign
*   The most common segmentation mistakes and exactly how to fix them

**Flowchart:**

The flowchart consists of five interconnected boxes, each representing a key concept:

*   **Segmentation:** This box is connected to the center of the flowchart and serves as the foundation for the other concepts.
*   **Personalization:** This box is linked to segmentation, indicating that personalization is a crucial step that follows segmentation.
*   **Automation:** Automation is connected to personalization, suggesting that automation enables efficient and targeted messaging.
*   **Activation:** Activation is linked to automation, implying that activation is the outcome of successful automation.
*   **Measurement:** Measurement is connected to activation, highlighting the importance of measuring the effectiveness of segmentation and personalization strategies.

The flowchart illustrates the interconnectedness of these concepts, demonstrating how they work together to create effective segmentation and personalization strategies. Overall, the slide provides a clear overview of the key concepts and their relationships, making it a valuable resource for students studying the material.

The image presents a summary of a lesson on segmentation in marketing, highlighting key takeaways and best practices. The main points are:

* **Segmentation Impact**
	+ Sending targeted messages to specific groups creates a 20%+ lift in open rates compared to unsegmented broadcasts.
	+ This is the single highest leverage variable in campaign performance.
* **The Segmentation Types**
	+ Use Demographic, Behavioral (what they did), Psychographic (how they think), and Lifecycle Stage (when they are/when to Start with behavior as a Lifecycle for the biggest immediate wins.
* **Go-to-Level Automation**
	+ Implement segmentation automatically using workflow triggers that apply tags and update pipeline stages based on user behavior, ensuring segments are always current.
* **Advanced Personalization**
	+ Go beyond "If it's first name" by using situational personalization (inferencing specific downloads/clicks) and predictive analytics (tracking intent) to map based on patterns).
* **Segment Architecture**
	+ Build your system first - map lifecycle stages, define behavioral tags, specify campaign filters, and plan personalization opportunities before launching any campaigns.
* **Top 3 Pitfalls**
	+ Immediate improvements come from: 
		- Always defaulting a target segment (stop broadcasting to all)
		- Adding exclusion filters/segments (all segments include)
		- Automating tag application via workflows

In summary, the image emphasizes the importance of segmentation in marketing, highlighting its impact on open rates and campaign performance. It outlines different types of segmentation, including demographic, behavioral, psychographic, and lifecycle stage, and provides tips for effective segmentation, such as implementing automation and personalization. Additionally, it warns against common pitfalls, including defaulting to a target segment, not using exclusion filters, and not automating tag application.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the four primary segmentation types used in marketing, each utilizing distinct data to categorize audiences. The title, "The 4 Segmentation Types," is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a subtitle that reads, "Each type uses different data to divide your list – the most powerful campaigns combine multiple types." 

The four segmentation types are:

**Demographic**: 
* Divides contacts by who they are
* Includes data such as:
	+ Age
	+ Income
	+ Marital status
	+ Affecting their needs, interests, and behaviors

**Behavioral**: 
* Divides contacts by what they have done
* Includes data such as:
	+ Previous purchases
	+ Website interactions
	+ Responding to previous campaigns

**Psychographic**: 
* Divides contacts by how they think and feel
* Includes data such as:
	+ Interests
	+ Attitudes
	+ Values

**Lifecycle Stage**: 
* Divides contacts by journey position
* Includes data such as:
	+ Leads
	+ Prospects
	+ First-time buyers
	+ Loyal customers

A central black circle with white text reads, "GROW YOUR CONTACT LIST." A black bar at the bottom of the image features white text that reads, "For 3x Increased and lifecycle stage segmentation deliver the biggest performance lifts for most marketing campaigns. Start with these two and then consider adding Demographic or Psychographic segments."

In the top-right corner, the word "Libre" appears in green text. The background of the image features a white base color, with each segmentation type displayed in a distinct colored box: blue for Demographic, green for Behavioral, yellow for Psychographic, and purple for Lifecycle Stage. This infographic provides a clear and concise visual representation of the four primary segmentation types, making it an effective tool for marketers seeking to understand and target their audiences effectively.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the four primary segmentation types used in marketing, each with its unique characteristics and applications. The title, "The 4 Segmentation Types," is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a brief description that highlights the importance of combining multiple types to create effective campaigns.

**Segmentation Types:**

*   **Demographic:** This type of segmentation focuses on dividing customers based on their demographic characteristics, such as:
    *   Gender
    *   Age
    *   Income
    *   Education
    *   Occupation
    *   Marital status
    *   Location
*   **Behavioral:** This type of segmentation categorizes customers based on their behaviors, including:
    *   Purchase history
    *   Product usage
    *   Website interactions
    *   Social media engagement
    *   Loyalty program participation
*   **Psychographic:** This type of segmentation explores customers' thoughts, feelings, and values, encompassing:
    *   Interests
    *   Lifestyles
    *   Personality traits
    *   Attitudes
    *   Opinions
*   **Lifecycle Stage:** This type of segmentation considers customers' positions in their life cycles, including:
    *   New customers
    *   Loyal customers
    *   Inactive customers

**Key Takeaways:**

*   Each segmentation type offers distinct data to divide your list.
*   The most effective campaigns often combine multiple types.
*   A black banner at the bottom provides additional information on how to leverage segmentation types to deliver the right message to customers.

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise introduction to the four primary segmentation types, highlighting their unique characteristics and applications. By understanding these segmentation types, marketers can create targeted and effective campaigns that resonate with their audience.

The image presents a table titled "Segmentation Meets Campaign Type," which outlines four distinct campaign types and their corresponding segmentation approaches. The table is divided into columns, each representing a different aspect of campaign segmentation.

*   **Campaign Type**
    *   The table lists four campaign types: Welcome, Nurture, Promotional, and Re-engagement.
    *   Each campaign type has a unique color-coded box with a brief description.
*   **Segmentation Trigger**
    *   This column explains the trigger for each campaign type.
    *   The triggers are:
        *   Liftoff Signup: first-time subscribers
        *   Behavioral: Completed Onboarding
        *   Behavioral: +1 week since last email
        *   Behavioral: No email activity
*   **Key Segment Filter**
    *   This column describes the key segment filter for each campaign type.
    *   The filters are:
        *   Tag: Subscribed, Tag: Welcome
        *   Tag: Subscribed, Email: < 7 days
        *   Offers are sent to profile remainder to convert
        *   Last email opened: < 30 days
*   **Personalization Opportunities**
    *   This column highlights personalization opportunities for each campaign type.
    *   The opportunities are:
        *   Lead to onboard
        *   Content loops to remind value, call to action
        *   Offers tailored to segment
        *   Subject: [Name], You're missed!
*   **Do Not Include**
    *   This column lists the groups that should be excluded from each campaign type.
    *   The excluded groups are:
        *   Existing customers
        *   Non-engaged contacts
        *   Any past award winners
        *   Cold inactive contacts
        *   Current customers
        *   Brand new leads
        *   Existing customers (with unpaid lead)
        *   Active openers
        *   Current customers
        *   High value contacts

In summary, the table provides a clear and concise guide to understanding the different campaign types and their corresponding segmentation approaches. By following this guide, marketers can create targeted and effective campaigns that resonate with their audience.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to personalization in sales, titled "Personalization Beyond 'Hi [First Name]'". The title is prominently displayed at the top, accompanied by a subtitle that reads, "First name personalization in sales takes more than just addressing them by their name - it's personalization changes what you say, not just how you address them." 

The infographic is divided into three main sections: **Surface Level**, **Situational**, and **Predictive**, each with a distinct color scheme and corresponding subcategories. 

*   **Surface Level** (gray)
    *   Uses the customer's level or job title or going
    *   Example: "The issue is new to your pitch"
*   **Situational** (blue)
    *   Referring to specific behaviors, usage, or current needs
    *   Example: "You've downloaded our 5-Day Sales Hustle Guide 12 days ago. It's likely you've just started dialing in. What challenges are you facing with prospecting right now?"
*   **Predictive** (green)
    *   Anticipating customer needs based on behavioral patterns, firmographic or technographic
    *   Example: "Hey / drop you a line about X..."

The infographic also features a table with four columns: **Identity**, **Lead Source**, **Lead Insight**, **Management**, and **Example**. 

*   **Identity** (gray)
    *   12% First Names
*   **Lead Source** (blue)
    *   CHECK How to learn more
*   **Lead Insight** (green)
    *   1 more - you've downloaded our 5-Day Sales Hustle Guide 12 days ago. It's likely you've just started dialing in. What challenges are you facing with prospecting right now? This week only, get 20% percent off our 12-week programs. That
*   **Management** (red)
    *   No example provided
*   **Example** (pink)
    *   No example provided

At the bottom of the image, a disclaimer states, "*Same offer. Same discount. Completely different conversational cadence & influence reached*". Overall, the infographic provides a clear and concise overview of the different levels of personalization in sales, highlighting the importance of going beyond simply using a customer's first name.

The image presents a slide from a presentation on building a segment architecture, titled "Building Your Segment Architecture - 5-Step Process" in black text at the top. The title is accompanied by a green line and the word "Libre" in green text to the right.

**The 5-Step Process:**

*   **Step 1: Map Journey Stages**
    *   Use one discrete segment per stage
    *   Create a stages map (e.g., awareness, consideration, purchase)
*   **Step 2: Define Behavioral Tags**
    *   Categorize with events to identify unique segments (e.g., visited homepage, viewed pricing)
*   **Step 3: Define Campaign Segments**
    *   Define segments for one campaign at a time (e.g., segment specification: 1st purchase, lapsed)
*   **Step 4: Map Personalization**
    *   Plan personalization by aligning segment-based attributes to campaigns
*   **Step 5: Build, Test, Refine**
    *   Build segments, test personalization layers, and analyze results from customer tests

**Additional Text:**

*   A black bar at the bottom of the image contains white text that reads: "A segment architecture allows you to design campaigns that resonate with every audience segment. You stop asking 'what should I say?' and start asking 'what is the right message to deliver to the right audience'."

The background of the image is white, providing a clean and clear visual representation of the 5-step process for building a segment architecture.

The image presents a list of seven common mistakes to avoid when segmenting campaigns, titled **"7 Segmentation Mistakes That Undermine Campaign Performance."** 

The mistakes and their fixes are as follows:

1. **Broadcasting to your entire list for every campaign** 
    * The mistake: Not segmenting
    * What it might look like: A brand-new product launch email sent to the entire list with no targeting or personalization
    * The fix: Divide the audience into segments to target personas or recent buyers, or use AI to automate dynamic content for each individual.

2. **No exclusion filters - or only inclusive filters**
    * The mistake: Not excluding
    * What it might look like: A Mother's Day campaign sent to your entire list
    * The fix: Every segmentation definition must include exclusions like NOT for people who have made a similar purchase in the last 6-12 months.

3. **Using only demographic segmentation - ignoring behavior**
    * The mistake: Not including behavior
    * What it might look like: Sending post-purchase follow-up survey to buyers who abandoned their carts
    * The fix: Use dynamic segmentation tools, behavior-based triggers, and AI to incorporate real-time engagement signals.

4. **Over segmenting into too many tiny lists**
    * The mistake: Creating too many segments
    * What it might look like: Creating individual campaigns for each segment with less than 50 people
    * The fix: Combine small segments with similar interests, and consolidate campaigns into manageable sizes.

5. **Promoting upsells to brand new customers - ignoring the buyer's journey**
    * The mistake: Ignoring the buyer's journey
    * What it might look like: Sending an upsell campaign to customers on their first purchase
    * The fix: State what stage your prospects are in the buyer's journey, e.g., first-time buyers, repeat customers, lapsed customers.

6. **Never updating or cleaning segments**
    * The mistake: Not cleaning data
    * What it might look like: Sending campaigns to a segment with outdated information
    * The fix: Configure regular data hygiene routines, like weekly or monthly data cleansing.

7. **Building segments manually instead of automating**
    * The mistake: Manual segment building
    * What it might look like: A marketer manually updating 500 contacts to manage a segment
    * The fix: Every campaign should be automated dynamically by a workflow. 

At the bottom of the image, it recommends **Quick Wins Minutes 1.5 & 7** - sending to everyone, missing exclusions, and building segments manually - as the **three most impactful fixes** for any email and SMS program that's underperforming.

The image presents a list of seven segmentation mistakes that can undermine campaign performance, along with their corresponding fixes. The title, "7 Segmentation Mistakes That Undermine Campaign Performance," is prominently displayed at the top.

**Mistakes and Fixes:**

*   **Broadcasting to your entire list for every campaign**
    *   Mistake: Failing to tailor campaigns to specific segments
    *   What it might look like: Sending the same email to everyone on your list
    *   Fix: Use data to inform your segmentation strategy and create targeted campaigns
*   **No exclusion filters - only include filters**
    *   Mistake: Not excluding inactive subscribers
    *   What it might look like: Constantly sending to someone who never buys or engages
    *   Fix: Every segmentation definition must include exclusions (like NOT for non-buyers)
*   **Using only demographic segmentation - ignoring behavioral**
    *   Mistake: Focusing solely on demographics
    *   What it might look like: Sending deals to students who bought from you 2+ years ago
    *   Fix: Using demographic segments, behavior, and preferences to create targeted campaigns
*   **Over segmenting into too many tiny lists**
    *   Mistake: Creating too many small segments
    *   What it might look like: Creating 35 segments to manage
    *   Fix: Consolidate campaigns into fewer lists (10-20) and consider consolidating into campaign types
*   **Prematurely optimizing to the campaign goal - ignoring the pre-optimization goals**
    *   Mistake: Focusing too early on conversion goals
    *   What it might look like: State your email subject might convert campaigns (e.g., buy now)
    *   Fix: Confirm your list/campaign execution aligns with your pre-optimization goals
*   **Not updating or cleaning segments**
    *   Mistake: Failing to regularly update segments
    *   What it might look like: Sending to outdated lists
    *   Fix: Regularly update segments to ensure accuracy
*   **Building segments manually instead of automating**
    *   Mistake: Manually building segments
    *   What it might look like: Using 500 contacts to manage 100,000
    *   Fix: Use automation to build segments

**Additional Information:**

*   A note at the bottom of the image highlights that Quick Wins Minutes 1, 5, and 7 - sending to everyone, missing exclusions, and building segments manually - are the most impactful fixes for any current program that's underperforming.
